summary:
Mughal Architecture's Golden Age (c. 1556–1658 AD) encompassed the reigns of [[event:2381|Akbar]], Jahangir, and [[event:2506|Shah Jahan]], during which the [[event:103|Mughal Empire]] produced the most extraordinary synthesis of Persian, Central Asian, Ottoman, and Indian architectural traditions, creating buildings of unparalleled grandeur and refinement that remain among the world's greatest monuments. The Mughal architectural vocabulary combined the Persian tradition of geometric ornamental tiles and large domed tombs with Indian traditions of carved stone screens (jali), temple-style brackets, and wide overhanging eaves, within a framework of symmetrical garden planning drawn from the Persian charbagh (four-part paradise garden) concept. Red sandstone dominated Akbar's buildings; Shah Jahan favored pure white marble with inlaid semi-precious stone work (pietra dura) of extraordinary delicacy.

The architectural program of the era was vast: Akbar built Agra Fort (1565–1573, massive red sandstone complex), Fatehpur Sikri (a complete new capital city, 1571–1585), and the Buland Darwaza (the world's highest gateway). Jahangir's reign produced the Shalimar Gardens of Lahore and Kashmir. Shah Jahan's legacy was the greatest: the [[event:2382|Taj Mahal]] (1632–1653), universally recognized as the finest single building of the Mughal period; the Red Fort of Delhi (1638–1648, his new capital); the Jama Masjid mosque; and the Peacock Throne, a jewel-encrusted throne of legendary magnificence (later looted by [[event:2447|Nader Shah]] in 1739). Mughal garden design influenced Safavid Persian and Ottoman Ottoman gardens, and the pietra dura stone inlay technique was introduced to India by Italian craftsmen and achieved its most extraordinary development in the Taj Mahal's decoration.
